title: Stripping paint from a wooden chair with a heat gun
subtitle: Remove old paint from a wooden chair frame using a heat gun and water-based stripper on mouldings
excerpt: How to strip layers of old paint from a solid wooden chair frame, using a heat gun and scraper on flat surfaces and water-based chemical stripper on carved or turned mouldings, then sanding ready for a new finish.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Household Guide (furniture). Collins Complete DIY Manual. The Workshop Companion (1913).

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of softening paint with a heat gun so it can be lifted cleanly from the surface with a scraper. The heat gun is held 50 to 100 mm from the surface and moved steadily: the paint blisters and wrinkles when ready. The scraper follows immediately while the paint is still hot. Overheating scorches the wood and raises grain; underheating means the paint tears rather than lifting clean.

A paste or gel paint stripper applied to carved, turned, or moulded surfaces where a heat gun cannot reach. Applied thickly and left for 30 to 60 minutes, it softens the paint film so it can be worked out of the profile with a wooden skewer, stiff brush, or shaped scraper. Water-based strippers (DCM-free) are safer than solvent-based formulations and less likely to raise the grain of the wood.

content.text: A wooden chair that has been painted many times over the years will have layers thick enough to obscure the profile of the mouldings and hide the quality of the timber beneath.
removes paint from the flat faces and rails quickly.
handles the turned legs and carved detail where the heat gun cannot follow the profile. Both methods together strip a chair frame cleanly.

Water-based paint stripper (gel or paste form) rated for multiple layers. Sandpaper in 80, 120, and 180 grit for the final clean-up. A soft brass wire brush for working stripper out of mouldings. Wooden skewers for cleaning inside turned grooves. Rubber gloves, eye protection, and adequate ventilation when using chemical stripper. A drop sheet under the chair to catch paint fragments.
Step 1, set up and heat-strip the flat faces

Lay the dust sheet and place the chair on top. Remove any fabric or upholstery from the chair before stripping: the heat gun will damage fabric and padding. Set the heat gun to medium heat (around 400°C). Hold it 50 to 100 mm from the surface and move it steadily in a small circular pattern until the paint begins to blister and wrinkle. Move the scraper in behind the heat gun immediately and lift the softened paint in one stroke. Drop the lifted paint onto the dust sheet.
Step 2, apply chemical stripper to mouldings and turned legs

Apply a thick coat of water-based gel stripper to all the turned and moulded sections. Stipple it into the grooves and recesses with a stiff brush so the stripper makes contact with all surfaces. Allow it to work for the manufacturer's stated dwell time (usually 30 to 60 minutes). Keep the area moist by covering with cling film: dried-out stripper does not work. Test a small section with a wooden skewer after 30 minutes.
Step 3, clean out the softened paint

Work the softened paint out of the mouldings with wooden skewers, the stiff brass brush, and a shaped scraper. Old brushes cut to a fan shape are useful for working into a round groove. Rinse the section with clean water and wipe down with a damp cloth to remove residue. For very thick paint layers, a second coat of stripper may be needed. Neutralise the surface with white spirit if the stripper instructions require it.

Allow the stripped surface to dry fully (24 hours). Sand the flat faces through 80, 120, and 180 grit, working along the grain. Use a folded piece of sandpaper to sand the mouldings by hand: press the paper into the profile rather than running it across. The goal is bare, smooth timber with the grain direction clear and no paint residue in the recesses. A sanding sealer or primer coat should be applied before repainting.

If the chair has a lead paint warning (any chair painted before 1990 may have lead-based primers), use chemical stripper throughout and do not use a heat gun. Heating lead paint releases lead oxide fumes. Collect and bag all stripped paint scrapings for disposal as hazardous waste rather than general rubbish.

A stripped and refinished chair frame needs a proper topcoat to protect the bare timber. Apply two coats of primer before any paint finish. For a natural timber look, apply one coat of sanding sealer, sand lightly, and then two coats of hard wax oil. A chair that is re-stripped and repainted rather than conditioned every few years will gradually lose detail in its mouldings: each full strip and repaint cycle takes about 0.5 mm of surface depth from the profiles.

attrs.symptom: Wood scorches or goes black under the heat gun
attrs.cause: The heat gun was held too close or moved too slowly; the surface temperature went above the paint softening point and reached the wood itself.
attrs.fix: Sand the scorched area back to clean wood with 80 grit before proceeding. Increase the distance from the heat gun to the surface and move faster. On a carved or thin section, switch to chemical stripper only.

attrs.symptom: Chemical stripper does not soften the paint after 60 minutes
attrs.cause: The stripper has dried before it had time to work; or the paint is a two-pack epoxy or hard catalysed finish that resists water-based strippers.
attrs.fix: Re-apply a thicker coat and cover with cling film to keep it moist. If the paint still resists after a second application, it is likely a modern catalysed finish. The only options are mechanical sanding or a professional solvent-based stripping service.
